spots.name: Fushimi Inari Taisha
spots.trivia: Famous for its thousands of vermilion torii gates winding up Mt. Inari. Dedicated to Inari, the god of rice and foxes.

spots.name: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
spots.trivia: A Zen Buddhist temple whose top two floors are covered in gold leaf. A UNESCO World Heritage Site.

spots.name: Nijo Castle
spots.trivia: Built in 1603 for Tokugawa Ieyasu. Famous for its 'nightingale floors' that squeak to detect intruders.

spots.name: Arashiyama Bamboo Grove
spots.trivia: A magical grove of towering bamboo stalks northwest of Kyoto. The sound of wind through the bamboo is nationally protected.

spots.name: Togetsu-kyo Bridge
spots.trivia: The 'Moon Crossing Bridge' spans the Oi River at Arashiyama. A symbol of Kyoto romanticism.

spots.name: Nishiki Market
spots.trivia: Known as 'Kyoto's Kitchen' — a narrow five-block shopping street lined with over 100 vendors selling traditional foods.

spots.name: Gion District
spots.trivia: Kyoto's most famous geisha district. Preserved machiya townhouses and cobblestone lanes evoke the Edo period.

spots.name: Ryoan-ji Temple
spots.trivia: Home to Japan's most celebrated Zen rock garden — 15 stones arranged so that at least one is always hidden from view.

spots.name: Yasaka Shrine
spots.trivia: Presides over Gion and hosts the Gion Matsuri, Kyoto's grandest festival dating back to 869 CE.

spots.name: Heian Jingu Shrine
spots.trivia: Built in 1895 to commemorate Kyoto's 1100th anniversary. Its vast garden recreates Heian-era landscape design.

spots.name: Philosopher's Path
spots.trivia: A stone path along a canal lined with hundreds of cherry trees, named after philosopher Nishida Kitaro who meditated here.

spots.name: Pontocho Alley
spots.trivia: A narrow lantern-lit alley between the Kamo River and Kawaramachi, famed for its traditional kaiseki restaurants.

spots.name: Kiyomizu-dera Temple
spots.trivia: Built on a hillside with a famous wooden stage extending over a cliff. Offers panoramic views of Kyoto below.

spots.name: Sanjusangen-do Temple
spots.trivia: Houses 1,001 life-sized wooden statues of Kannon, the goddess of mercy. The longest wooden structure in Japan.

spots.name: Ninna-ji Temple
spots.trivia: A UNESCO site with a five-story pagoda. Famous for late-blooming dwarf cherry trees called Omuro cherries.

spots.name: Daitoku-ji Complex
spots.trivia: A Zen Buddhist complex comprising 22 sub-temples with some of Kyoto's finest rock gardens.

spots.name: Fure-ai Kan Museum
spots.trivia: A museum in the heart of Kyoto preserving traditional arts, crafts, and the culture of the Nishijin textile weavers.

spots.name: Tenryu-ji Garden
spots.trivia: A UNESCO World Heritage garden that uses 'borrowed scenery' — the Arashiyama mountains frame its central pond.

spots.name: Shimogamo Shrine
spots.trivia: One of Kyoto's oldest shrines, surrounded by the Tadasu no Mori primeval forest. Hosts the Aoi Matsuri ceremony.

spots.name: Kyoto Imperial Palace
spots.trivia: The former ruling seat of Japan's Imperial family. The grounds are now a public park open for strolling year-round.
